meta.sourceAcknowledgement: Adapted from the NZ Anaesthetic Crisis Handbook (Adam Hollingworth et al., v3.3 Feb 2023) and the guidelines it draws on: ANZAAG, AAGBI, NZRC, Starship Protocols, vortexapproach.org, CCDHB/Hutt Valley clinical protocols, ESA Emergency Quick Reference Guide, Wellington ICU Drug Manual. legend.red: Emergency / doing tasks — work through in order
legend.yellow: Thinking tasks — diagnostic or further information

cards.title: Airway Management – Vortex
cards.priority: Oxygenation in the green zone
doing: Always have a safe airway strategy ready (e.g. awake fibreoptic intubation, call ENT/surgeon)
Pre-oxygenate all patients
Consider passive apnoeic oxygenation via nasal cannula during RSI
Remove cricoid pressure early if it is impeding the view
Address all airway attempts using the Vortex Approach (vortexapproach.org – Dr Chrimes & Dr Fritz)
If first airway plan fails: get the difficult intubation trolley and extra help
Goal: restore oxygenation and reach the Green Zone (EtCO₂ present & safe SpO₂ – i.e. an SpO₂ that would cause no harm if it persisted 15 min)
Try the three lifelines – bag/mask, supraglottic airway (SGA), endotracheal tube (ETT) – in any order
For each lifeline: at least 1 attempt, no more than 3 (a 4th is allowed only if a genuine game-changer becomes available, e.g. new kit, expert help)
A best effort at any lifeline must include full muscle relaxation
Optimisations – Bag/Mask: dentures in, two-hand vice grip, cuff inflation/deflation, OPA/NPA, change mask size/type, suction, paralysis
Optimisations – SGA: pull tongue forward, twist to insert, cuff inflation/deflation, insert with laryngoscope/bougie, change size/type, suction, paralysis
Optimisations – ETT: dentures out, laryngeal manipulation, remove cricoid pressure, lift epiglottis, bougie/stylet, change tube size/blade, video laryngoscope, suction, paralysis

If in the Green Zone, plan for ongoing safety: Maintain (consider waking – sugammadex, naloxone), Convert (fibreoptic ETT through SGA or surgical airway), or Replace (leave Green Zone and re-enter the Vortex)

With an unsuccessful best effort at any lifeline, escalate CICO status: Ready (get CICO kit, designate proceduralist) → Set (ready equipment, palpate landmarks) → Go (optimise position and start CICO Rescue if not in Green Zone after 3 lifeline best efforts)

cards.title: CICO Rescue
cards.priority: Oxygenation with stable SpO₂ > 90%
doing: Dedicated team member continues attempting supraglottic oxygenation
Pull patient up the bed so the head extends over a pillow
Decide and share your intended technique with the team early – three options:

1. Cannula cricothyroidotomy (palpable anatomy): CICO pack (14G cannula, 5 mL syringe with 2 mL saline, rapid-O₂ insufflation device); secure the cricoid cartilage and aspirate while advancing the saline-filled cannula; success = free aspiration of air – never let go of the cannula

Connect the rapid-O₂ device to the cannula and the machine's auxiliary O₂ port at 10 L/min: 1st breath 6 sec (1000 mL) – look for chest rise/fall; wait 20 sec for SpO₂ rise, or until SpO₂ starts to drop 5% from peak; 2nd breath 3 sec (500 mL), repeating only after the same wait; abandon the technique if there is no SpO₂ rise after the 2nd breath or any doubt
Convert to a size 5 Melker airway using the Seldinger technique

2. Scalpel–bougie (palpable anatomy): prepare gauze/suction for blood; horizontal stab incision through the cricothyroid membrane with a 10-blade scalpel; rotate the blade to vertical (caudad) and pass the bougie alongside it; remove the scalpel and railroad a size 6 ETT over the bougie

3. Scalpel–finger–cannula/scalpel (non-palpable anatomy): prepare for significant blood; vertical midline 8–10 cm incision through skin and subcutaneous tissue; blunt-dissect with both hands down to the airway and secure the cartilage; insert a cannula or scalpel through the cricothyroid membrane or trachea; then follow step 1 or 2 above to oxygenate
thinking: Choice of first method is operator preference – decide and practise (mentally or in simulation) before a crisis
If the first method fails, move to the alternative method immediately
If there is no palpable anatomy, the scalpel–finger method is recommended

cards.title: Laryngospasm
cards.priority: Break laryngospasm and maintain SpO₂
doing: Ask the surgeon to stop
Get drugs and airway equipment

Manual procedures: remove the LMA and clear the airway; consider an OP/NP airway; jaw thrust with CPAP 30 cmH₂O – do not give a positive-pressure breath; apply bilateral, painful, inward pressure at Larson's point (immediately behind the ear lobule); in children, consider gentle chest compressions, which may be more effective than other manual manoeuvres

If SpO₂ is stable and >92%, try low-dose muscle relaxation: propofol 20% of the induction dose, or suxamethonium 35 mg IV (0.5 mg/kg) — note that obese, paediatric, or acutely unwell patients desaturate quickly and intubation may be a better first move
If SpO₂ is dropping or <92%, give full-dose muscle relaxation as soon as possible
Consider atropine for bradycardia
Consider gastric decompression after the event
thinking: Laryngospasm will eventually break with time and hypoxia, but may be preceded by bradycardia, cardiac arrest, aspiration, or pulmonary oedema
Hypoxia can occur rapidly in paediatric, obese, and acutely unwell patients
Pre-prepare IV and IM suxamethonium doses in higher-risk cases

cards.title: Bronchospasm
cards.priority: SpO₂ > 95% with peak airway pressures < 40 cmH₂O
doing: Inform the surgeon; minimise surgical stimulation
Check airway position, the EtCO₂ trace (severe bronchospasm can present with low/absent EtCO₂), and airway pressures
Manually ventilate to confirm high pressures and ensure adequate tidal volume
Deepen anaesthesia; if using desflurane, switch to an alternative agent
Emergency drug therapy: inhaled salbutamol via the circuit, inhaled ipratropium via the circuit, IV salbutamol slow bolus (repeat at 10 min), IV adrenaline 0.1–0.5 mL of 1:10,000
Optimise ventilator settings: pressure-control mode, long expiratory phase, low respiratory rate, low PEEP, small tidal volumes, intermittent disconnection
Other bolus adjuncts: magnesium, ketamine, hydrocortisone, aminophylline
If no improvement, use infusions of salbutamol, adrenaline, or aminophylline
Place an arterial line and take serial blood gases
thinking: Always consider other causes of high airway pressure: anaphylaxis, laryngospasm (on an LMA), tube position, chest wall rigidity, pneumothorax, acute pulmonary oedema
Permissive hypercapnia may be needed to reduce airway pressures
Assess response by falling airway pressures, blood gases, and improving EtCO₂ trace

drugs.bolus: 100–250 mcg slow bolus, may repeat at 10 min
drugs.infusion: 5 mg in 50 mL saline, infuse 0–10 mL/hr
drugs.peds: 10 mcg/kg over 2 min (max 500 mcg single dose); repeat at 10 min; infusion 5–10 mcg/kg/min for 1 hr then 1–2 mcg/kg/min
0.1 mL/kg (max 5 mL) in 50 mL saline over 20 min

drugs.infusion: 50 mg in 50 mL at 35 mL/hr
drugs.peds: load 10 mg/kg over 1 hr diluted to 50 mL; infusion varies by age

drugs.infusion: 5 mg in 50 mL saline, 0–20 mL/hr (not generally recommended in children)

cards.title: Aspiration
cards.priority: Minimise aspiration while maintaining SpO₂
doing: Call for help from surgical team members immediately
If practical, move the patient to head-down, left-lateral position as soon as possible
Remove the LMA/OP airway and suction the pharynx

If time allows and SpO₂ is stable >97%: cricoid pressure (if not actively vomiting), suxamethonium IV, intubate, suction through the ETT with the largest possible catheter, only then ventilate with 100% O₂
If SpO₂ is dropping or <90%: do not delay oxygenation regardless of particulate matter — bag-mask ventilate with 100% O₂, or give manual breaths via the ETT with 100% O₂
Consider bronchoscopy
Consider abandoning surgery
Pass an NG tube at the earliest convenience
thinking: Monitor the patient for 2 hours post-event in PACU – if asymptomatic with normal vital signs and a normal CXR, ICU admission is unlikely to be required
Steroids and antibiotics are not routinely used in aspiration

cards.title: Adult Cardiac Arrest – VF/VT
cards.priority: Early defibrillation
doing: Ask surgeons to stop (if appropriate)
Start chest compressions at 100/min, monitor EtCO₂, ensure full chest recoil
Attach the defibrillator; shock immediately at 200 J (or maximum setting)
100% O₂; stop all anaesthetic agents
If using a mask/LMA: 30 compressions : 2 breaths. If the ETT is patent and secure: ventilate at 10 breaths/min and do not pause compressions
Follow 2-minute cycles: charge defibrillator → rhythm check → shock → restart compressions
Give adrenaline 1 mg immediately after the 2nd shock, then every 4 min
Give amiodarone 300 mg immediately after the 3rd shock
If the ECG shows an organised QRS complex, go to the Asystole/PEA card to check for pulse/ROSC
Read out and consider reversible causes (see Thinking)
Get the ultrasound to help rule out causes, if skilled

If ROSC, consider post-resuscitation care: abandon surgery, urgent cardiology referral (consider PCI), full ABCDE assessment, blood gases, 12-lead ECG; avoid SpO₂ >99%, hyperglycaemia (>10 mmol/L), hypercarbia, and temperature >37.5 °C for 72 hours
thinking: Reversible causes: hypoxia; hypovolaemia or haemorrhage; hypo/hyperthermia; electrolyte/metabolic disturbance (K⁺, Mg²⁺, glucose, pH, Ca²⁺); tension pneumothorax; cardiac tamponade; anaphylaxis and toxins (opioids, local anaesthetics, calcium-channel or beta blockers, other drug errors); cardiac or pulmonary thrombosis; if pregnant, perform manual uterine displacement and start preparations to deliver the baby by 5 minutes

drugs.drug: Adrenaline IV (follow all drugs with a 20 mL flush)
Calcium chloride IV (hyperkalaemia or CCB overdose)
Sodium bicarbonate 8.4% IV (hyperkalaemia, TCA overdose, or low pH)
drugs.bolus: 50 mL slow push, may repeat every 2 min until pH 7.45–7.55
7 mL bolus (0.1 mL/kg), may repeat every 10 min (max 0.3 mL/kg)

drugs.infusion: 1000 mL/hr (15 mL/kg/hr)

drugs.bolus: 50 mg slow push, may repeat at 15 min – be prepared for prolonged CPR up to 60 min

cards.title: Adult Cardiac Arrest – Asystole / PEA
cards.priority: Good quality CPR
doing: Ask surgeons to stop all vagal stimuli
Start chest compressions at 100/min, monitor EtCO₂, ensure full chest recoil
Attach the defibrillator
100% O₂; stop all anaesthetic agents
If using a mask/LMA: 30 compressions : 2 breaths. If the ETT is patent and secure: ventilate at 10 breaths/min, do not pause compressions
Follow 2-minute cycles: charge defibrillator → rhythm and pulse check → restart compressions
Give adrenaline 1 mg immediately, then every 4 min
If the ECG shows VF/VT, go to that card
In asystole, if P waves are present consider pacing
Read out and consider reversible causes
Get the ultrasound to help rule out causes, if skilled
If ROSC, consider post-resuscitation care as for VF/VT

cards.title: Paediatric Cardiac Arrest
cards.priority: Ensure adequate oxygenation and good CPR
doing: Ask surgeons to stop all vagal stimuli
100% O₂, stop anaesthetic agents, give 2 breaths
Start chest compressions at 120/min, monitor EtCO₂, ensure full chest recoil
If using a mask/LMA: 15 compressions : 2 breaths. If the ETT is patent and secure: ventilate at 15 breaths/min, do not pause compressions
Attach the defibrillator
Ensure IV access; if none, establish intraosseous access without delay
Follow 2-minute cycles: charge defibrillator at 4 J/kg → rhythm check ± shock → restart compressions
If VF/VT: shock immediately, then every cycle; give adrenaline 10 mcg/kg straight after the 2nd shock then every 4 min; give amiodarone 5 mg/kg straight after the 3rd shock
If asystole/PEA: give adrenaline as soon as possible, then every 4 min
Atropine 20 mcg/kg is only used for vagal-associated bradycardia
Read out and consider reversible causes
Get the ultrasound to rule out causes, if skilled
If ROSC, follow adult post-resuscitation care
thinking: Reversible causes (most common in bold): hypoxia & vagal tone; hypovolaemia/haemorrhage/anaphylaxis; hypo/hyperthermia; electrolyte/metabolic disturbance; tension pneumothorax; cardiac tamponade; anaphylaxis & toxins; thrombosis

drugs.peds: 4 J/kg; use attenuated paediatric pads for <8 yr if available
Preterm <1.5 kg = 2.5; preterm 1.5–3 kg = 3; <1 yr = 3.5–4; >1 yr = (age/4)+4
IV = 10 mcg/kg (0.1 mL/kg of 1:10,000); ETT = 100 mcg/kg (0.1 mL/kg of 1:1,000)

cards.title: Anaphylaxis
cards.priority: Cease triggers, give adrenaline and IV fluid
doing: Get the anaphylaxis box (or follow ANZAAG task cards)
Stop or remove causative agents (drugs, blood products, latex, chlorhexidine, etc.)
Consider early intubation (risk of airway oedema)
Ensure large-bore IV access; consider intraosseous access if none
Treat based on grade of anaphylaxis (see grading table)
Give IV adrenaline and fluids as soon as possible (if no IV: IM adrenaline 0.5 mL of 1:1,000, repeat every 5 min)
Repeat adrenaline and fluid boluses every 1–2 minutes as required
If more than 3 adrenaline boluses are given, start an adrenaline infusion
Refractory bronchospasm: salbutamol puffs → IV bolus → infusion
Refractory hypotension: adrenaline infusion → repeat fluid bolus → noradrenaline ± vasopressin infusion
Monitor treatment success: MAP, SpO₂, airway pressures, EtCO₂ waveform, echo
Place an arterial line
Consider abandoning surgery
Once stabilised: dexamethasone 12 mg (0.6 mg/kg)
thinking: Consider differentials such as tension pneumothorax or auto-PEEP
Collect tryptase levels (yellow-top tube) at time of event, 4 hours, and 24 hours

drugs.peds: 2 mcg/kg (dilute 1 mg in 50 mL = 20 mcg/mL, give 0.1 mL/kg)
4–10 mcg/kg (0.2–0.5 mL/kg of the 20 mcg/mL dilution)

drugs.drug: IV adrenaline by grade — Grade 4 (cardiac arrest)
Adrenaline / Noradrenaline infusion (no CVL needed to start)

drugs.peds: 0.15 mg/kg made to 50 mL, infuse 1–40 mL/hr

drugs.bolus: 20 units in 20 mL saline, bolus 1 mL

cards.title: Myocardial Ischaemia – Intraoperative
cards.priority: Decrease myocardial O₂ consumption and increase myocardial O₂ supply
doing: Titrate inspired O₂ to a normal SpO₂ of 97–99% (PaO₂ 80–100 mmHg)
Check depth of anaesthesia; ensure adequate analgesia
Control heart rate, target 60–80 bpm: minimise surgical stimulation where possible; esmolol or metoprolol boluses titrated to effect

Target MAP 65–75 mmHg: if MAP <65, use vasopressors or ephedrine cautiously; if refractory, consider an inotrope (e.g. dobutamine) or inodilator (e.g. milrinone), or cardiothoracic referral for an intra-aortic balloon pump; if MAP >75, use a GTN infusion
Avoid hypovolaemia — replace surgical losses and transfuse to Hb 80–90 g/L
If ongoing signs of ischaemia, start a GTN infusion regardless of MAP and support MAP with drugs/IABP as required
Expedite the end of surgery

cards.title: Massive Haemorrhage
cards.priority: Volume replacement and good teamwork
doing: IV access: two 16G cannulae ± rapid infusion catheter (use the largest IV available, remove any extension)
Talk to the surgeon — all efforts being made for surgical control of bleeding (compression, packing, direct pressure, arterial/aortic clamping)?
Give tranexamic acid: 1 g (standard & obstetric) or 2 g (trauma)
Call the blood bank: "I am requesting a (Crimson/Standard/Obstetric) Stat Pack"

If ongoing massive bleeding with shock: call the blood bank and state "I am activating a (Crimson/Standard/Obstetric) Massive Haemorrhage Protocol"; assemble a team with clear roles (transfusion coordinator, MHP runner, blood checkers, people to hang blood); with all MHP packs, give IV calcium chloride 10 mL via a fast, separate IV
Set up a rapid infusion device ± cell saver
Insert an arterial line
Use permissive hypotension — MAP 55–65 mmHg until haemostasis is established (except head injuries, where the target is MAP 80–90 mmHg)
Aggressively keep the patient warm (>36 °C): warm fluids, warm theatre, forced-air warmer
Check bloods every 30 min: coagulation studies (TEG if available), FBC, blood gas, ionised calcium
Stand down the MHP once clinically stable and change to targeted transfusion
thinking: Code Crimson (trauma): use an ABC score ≥2 as the threshold for calling the stat pack — 1 point each for penetrating mechanism, SBP ≤90 mmHg, positive eFAST, HR ≥120 bpm
Smaller centres should only give platelets if the platelet count is <75 ×10⁹/L
A new platelet infusion set is preferred but not essential
Do not give calcium chloride through the same line as blood products; keep a separate IV running crystalloid quickly

cards.title: Air / Gas Embolism
cards.priority: Restore cardio-respiratory stability
doing: 100% oxygen
Stop nitrous oxide

Stop the source of air/gas entry: lower the surgical site below the level of the heart and flood with irrigation fluid; search for an entry point such as an open venous line; in neurosurgery, consider intermittent jugular venous compression
Place the patient head-down, left tilt
Remove pneumoperitoneum if in use
If a CVL is in place, aspirate the line
Consider chest compressions at 100/min even if not in arrest — this is known to help break up volumes of air
Aim for MAP >65 mmHg: assess fluid responsiveness with a 500 mL crystalloid bolus; use vasoactive medications such as noradrenaline, adrenaline, or dobutamine
Consider early transoesophageal echo to rule out other causes
Consider referral for hyperbaric oxygen therapy
thinking: Signs: respiratory — falling EtCO₂ (most sensitive), falling SpO₂, pulmonary oedema, bronchospasm; cardiovascular — shock, tachycardia, rising pulmonary artery pressures, cardiovascular collapse
Use of PEEP is controversial — may increase the risk of paradoxical air embolism through a patent foramen ovale (present in 10–30% of the population)
Hyperbaric oxygen treatment up to 6 hours post-event may improve outcomes in paradoxical air embolism

cards.title: Local Anaesthetic Toxicity
cards.priority: Good quality CPR and early intralipid
doing: Stop administering LA and get the LA toxicity box (or follow AAGBI task cards)

If signs of cardiac output: consider intubation; ventilate if needed (aim EtCO₂ ~30 mmHg); confirm IV access; consider giving 20% intralipid early (bolus then infusion); if arrhythmia, use standard protocols (consider amiodarone 300 mg slow IV — avoid lignocaine, use beta-blockers with caution); support MAP with fluids and vasopressors; treat seizures with midazolam IV, repeated every minute (max 10 mg) — if refractory, perform RSI

If cardiac arrest: start CPR and be prepared to continue for up to 60 minutes; give 20% IV intralipid bolus, may repeat every 5 min up to twice; infusion 1000 mL/hr neat intralipid, double the rate at 5 min if no improvement, do not exceed the maximum dose of 840 mL; mobilise the cardiopulmonary bypass/ECMO team if available; send a blood gas and keep pH >7.25 with sodium bicarbonate 8.4% (may repeat every 2 min, ensure adequate ventilation)

Temporary pacing may be required for symptomatic bradycardia

cards.title: Malignant Hyperthermia
cards.priority: Early recognition, removal of triggers, dantrolene
doing: Recognise the problem — if in doubt, treat
Call for the MH trolley (or distribute/follow MH task cards)
Delegate and organise help into teams
Stop volatile agents and wash out the circuit with 100% oxygen at 15 L/min; switch to TIVA
Add charcoal filters to the circuit; change soda lime if easy — do not waste time changing the machine or circuit
Give IV dantrolene and get more from the on-call pharmacist; reconstitute each vial into a 60 mL syringe with water; repeat every 10 min until control is achieved
Increase monitoring: arterial line ± CVL with serial bloods (gas every 30 min, coagulation, CK); urinary catheter aiming for output >2 mL/kg/hr; core temperature probe (rectal or bladder)

Treat complications: temperature >38.5 °C — refrigerated IV fluids (and intraperitoneal if surgical access), surface ice, cold theatre; pH <7.2 — ventilate EtCO₂ to 30 cmH₂O ± sodium bicarbonate; K⁺ >7 or ECG changes — calcium chloride, insulin-dextrose infusion, salbutamol puffs; arrhythmias — defibrillate, consider amiodarone ± lignocaine ± esmolol; MAP <65 mmHg — start a noradrenaline infusion
Consider abandoning surgery and ICU referral
thinking: Rapid diagnosis: blood gas shows a mixed respiratory and metabolic acidosis

Signs suggesting MH — Early: rising EtCO₂, masseter spasm, rising HR/arrhythmia. Developing: rising temperature/sweating, CVS instability, falling pH, rising K⁺. Late: cola-coloured urine, coagulopathy with very high CK, cardiac arrest

cards.title: Fire — Airway or Patient
cards.priority: Airway: disconnect circuit and flood with saline. Patient: recognise fire and extinguish
doing: AIRWAY FIRE — stop the ignition source (laser/diathermy); turn off oxygen and disconnect the breathing circuit from the airway device
Extinguish: flood with saline (50 mL into the mouth, 10–20 mL down the ETT) or use a CO₂ extinguisher (safe in the airway)
Remove the airway device and keep it for inspection (only leave the ETT in place if difficult intubation and very low risk of fire extending into the tube)
Remove flammable material from the mouth — packs, gauze, sponges; retrieve debris with a Yankauer or large-bore suction catheter
Convert to TIVA
Restart ventilation only once the fire is fully extinguished (wait 1–3 min if SpO₂ allows): bag-mask initially, prepare for early re-intubation, use the lowest possible oxygen for normal SpO₂
If unable to re-intubate: emergency — infraglottic technique; urgent — call ENT for tracheostomy
Terminate or expedite the end of surgery
Post-crisis: bronchoscopic exam to assess mucosal airway damage; do not extubate, refer to ICU
PATIENT FIRE — stop any flow of oxygen or nitrous near/into the fire; remove all drapes and flammable material
Extinguish with saline, a fire blanket, or CO₂ extinguisher (safe in wounds and around electrical equipment) — do not use alcohol-based liquids or any liquid on/around electrical equipment
If fire persists: activate the fire alarm, turn off the gas supply to the room, evacuate
thinking: To reduce airway fire risk: lowest possible oxygen, avoid nitrous, saline in ETT/LMA cuffs, wet throat packs, laser-resistant ETT with methylene blue in the proximal cuff and saline in the distal cuff for LASER surgery
To reduce patient fire risk: allow skin preps to fully dry, use moistened sponges/gauzes near ignition sources

cards.title: Maternal Collapse
cards.priority: Good CPR, diagnose the cause, prepare for delivery
doing: Review all infusions/medications recently administered
Consider haemorrhage (possibly concealed) — call the blood bank for an Obstetric Stat Pack

If no cardiac output: call 777 and declare 'maternal cardiac arrest'; start preparations to deliver the baby now (peri-mortem caesarean or instrumental delivery); remove all foetal monitoring; start CPR, apply the defibrillator, check rhythm, and follow VF/VT or Asystole/PEA as appropriate; ensure IV access, consider IO if none; consider reversible causes and treat as soon as possible

Maternal-specific tasks during CPR: lift the uterus skyward and displace to the left; intubate early and ventilate with an EtCO₂ target of 30 mmHg; perform chest compressions higher on the chest and push deeper; if the patient is >24 weeks and there is no rapid ROSC, start immediate preparations to deliver the baby within 5 minutes

If peri- or post-arrest: start standard peri-arrest care, supporting ABCs as appropriate (intubate early); consider reversible causes; continue lifting the uterus skyward and displacing left if the baby is not yet delivered

cards.title: Neonatal Life Support
cards.priority: Dry the baby, oxygenate, reassess every 30 seconds
doing: Pre-set the Neopuff: gas supply at 10 L/min, PEEP 5, PIP 30 cmH₂O; have a heater and suction ready
In the first minute: vigorously dry the baby and apply warm, dry towels
Work in 30-second cycles — intervene, then reassess: tone (upper and lower limbs); heart rate (via SpO₂ probe or stethoscope); respiratory rate (gasping or apnoeic?)
If HR >100, good tone, regular respiration: give routine care
If well except for increased work of breathing: open the airway and give 5 cmH₂O CPAP with room air

If any of HR <100, poor tone, or gasping/apnoeic: start ventilating with EtCO₂ monitoring — fine-tune the neutral head position with jaw thrust; start with room air, increase O₂ every 30 sec if no improvement (40→60→80→100%); give 5 inflation breaths of 2–3 sec at PIP 30 cmH₂O; once adequate chest rise, ventilate at RR 40–60/min with PIP 15–20 cmH₂O

If HR <60: 100% O₂, consider an LMA or intubation if skilled; start chest compressions at 100/min (two-thumb technique with a second person for the airway is preferred); use a ratio of 3 compressions : 1 breath
If HR remains <60: give 1:10,000 adrenaline based on gestation; umbilical venous catheter is preferred (one vein, two arteries); consider an umbilical saline bolus of 10 mL/kg
thinking: If preterm, use lower inflation pressures: 28–32 weeks = 25/5; <28 weeks = 20/5
For significant meconium delivery, only suction a flat baby prior to oxygenating
Place an NG tube to decompress the stomach if ventilation is difficult
An assistant can place the SpO₂ probe on the right arm at any point — targets: 1 min 60–70%, 2 min 65–85%, 3 min 70–90%, 4 min 75–90%, 5 min 80–90%, 10 min 85–90%

drugs.peds: Full term = 200 mcg IM; otherwise 10 mcg/kg IM/IV
Term: 3–3.5 mm, length at lips 9 cm; preterm: 2.5 mm, length at lips 7 cm

cards.title: Total / High Spinal
cards.priority: Rapid management of ABCs
doing: If on the delivery suite: call 777 and declare "obstetric & neonatal emergency"
Review all infusions/medications and consider reversible causes

If no cardiac output: start CPR, apply the defibrillator, check rhythm and follow VF/VT or Asystole/PEA; if obstetric, follow maternal-specific CPR tasks (uterine displacement, early intubation with EtCO₂ target 30 mmHg, higher/deeper compressions, prepare for delivery by 5 min if no rapid ROSC and >24 weeks); total-spinal-specific tasks — give adrenaline 1 mg as soon as possible and start early rapid infusion of 2–3 L fluid

If respiratory arrest, distress, or falling SpO₂: elevate the head of the bed to 30°; assist ventilation with 100% O₂ via bag-mask while preparing to RSI; consider induction with midazolam, alfentanil, and suxamethonium

If cardiovascularly unstable (falling HR and MAP): elevate the legs, rapidly infuse 2–3 L fluid; if obstetric, lift the uterus skyward and displace left; if HR <60 give atropine, repeat if required; give a vasopressor depending on HR, repeat as required; for refractory hypotension use adrenaline boluses ± infusion
thinking: Diagnosis is clear if there is a witnessed, rapidly ascending block following a neuraxial procedure

If unwitnessed collapse, consider other causes: vasovagal, haemorrhage (external or concealed), LA toxicity, amniotic fluid embolism, magnesium toxicity, IVC compression, massive pulmonary embolus, drug error

drugs.infusion: 5 mg in 50 mL saline, infuse 0–20 mL/hr

cards.title: Post Partum Haemorrhage
cards.priority: Prepare for massive, rapid blood loss
doing: Two 16G IV cannulae — consider intraosseous access or a rapid infusion catheter
If out of theatre: call 777 and declare an "obstetric emergency"
Encourage surgical control of uterine tone and bleeding
Review with the surgeon every 10 minutes: diagnosis and plan
If massive bleeding with shock: call the blood bank — "I am requesting an Obstetric Stat Pack"; give 1 g tranexamic acid slow push

If ongoing massive bleeding with shock: call the blood bank — "I am activating the Obstetric MHP"; repeat 1 g tranexamic acid; refer to generic MHP steps (teamwork, regular calcium, rapid infusion device, arterial line, permissive hypotension, warming, bloods every 30 min)

Use oxytocics for uterine atony: oxytocin slow IV push, then an infusion; ergometrine IM (avoid if hypertensive); carboprost IM (avoid if asthmatic, may repeat every 15 min, max 8 doses); misoprostol PR/vaginal
Perform RSI to enable surgical control (spinal only if haemodynamically normal): induction with ketamine and suxamethonium; maintenance with TIVA or volatile/nitrous
thinking: Major causes of PPH: tone (75%); tissue/retained placenta (15%); trauma/laceration (5–10%); thrombosis/coagulopathy; splenic artery rupture (rare)
Surgical control options: pre-theatre — uterine massage, bimanual compression, aortal compression; intra-op — BAKRI balloon, B-Lynch suture, aortal compression, artery ligation, hysterectomy

drugs.infusion: 40 units in 500 mL saline, infuse at 125 mL/hr

drugs.bolus: 250 mcg IM (avoid if asthmatic), may repeat every 15 min, max 8 doses
Metaraminol 1 mg; phenylephrine 100 mcg; adrenaline 10–100 mcg titrated

drugs.infusion: 5 mg in 50 mL saline, infuse 10–20 mL/hr, preferably via CVC

cards.title: Peri-Partum Seizure
cards.priority: Oxygenation, magnesium, and treating hypertension
doing: Call 777 and state "obstetric emergency"
Call for the eclampsia box
Give O₂ 15 L/min via a non-rebreathe facemask
Apply monitoring: SpO₂, ECG, NIBP
Start a timer to measure the length of the seizure (eclamptic seizures normally self-terminate)

Maximise patient safety while displacing the gravid uterus if antenatal: pillows and covered bed sides; depending on staff safety, lift the uterus up and to the left or place the patient in full left lateral
Prepare and give magnesium (49.3%) as soon as possible: loading dose, then a maintenance infusion; if no IV, give IM into each gluteal region; if a repeat seizure occurs, give the rescue dose

If ongoing seizures or a seizure lasting >10 minutes, escalate treatment: midazolam IV bolus repeated every minute (max 10 mg), or high-concentration midazolam nasally/IM if no IV; then perform RSI and refer to ICU

Post-seizure: review ABCs and check blood glucose; send blood tests (FBC, LFTs, U&Es, uric acid, coagulation screen, magnesium, group & hold); consider aspiration risk; if BP >160/100 mmHg, consider labetalol and/or hydralazine; restrict total fluid input to 80 mL/hr and monitor hourly urine via catheter
If antenatal: discuss delivery planning with the obstetric team
Consider other causes of seizure besides eclampsia, discuss with neurology
thinking: Check reflexes, sedation score, and vital signs — initially every 30 min, then hourly

Serum magnesium levels are only needed if there is concurrent renal dysfunction; therapeutic level is 2–4 mmol/L; send a sample 1 hour after starting maintenance, repeat every 4 hours if there is concern
If concerned about magnesium toxicity: stop the infusion and give calcium chloride 10% 5 mL IV push

drugs.bolus: 8 mL with 12 mL saline, infuse at 120 mL/hr

drugs.infusion: add 16 mL to 100 mL saline, infuse at 14.5 mL/hr

drugs.bolus: 4 mL with 6 mL saline, slow IV push over 5 min
2 mg IV bolus, repeat every minute (max 10 mg); or 5 mg/mL nasal 2 mL via atomiser, or IM 2 mL into deltoid if no IV
4 mL (neat 5 mg/mL) over 2 min, repeat every 10 min (max 3 doses)
Dilute to 1 mg/mL, give 5 mL slow push, repeat every 20 min

cards.title: Amniotic Fluid Embolism
cards.priority: Recognition and aggressive resuscitation
doing: Get senior help or call 777 and declare an "obstetric ± neonatal emergency"

For all: start treatment for haemorrhage and coagulopathy — call the blood bank stating "I am requesting an Obstetric Stat Pack" and "I am activating the Obstetric MHP"; give IV tranexamic acid 1 g slow push, repeat 30 minutes later; send urgent bloods including FBC, coagulation studies, and TEG if available

If no cardiac output: start CPR and consider reversible causes; if antenatal, perform maternal-specific CPR tasks (remove foetal monitoring, lift uterus skyward and displace left, intubate early with EtCO₂ target <30 mmHg, higher/deeper compressions, prepare for delivery within 5 min if no rapid ROSC)

If signs of cardiac output: start resuscitation — ensure a patent airway and consider early intubation; address oxygenation with high-flow oxygen, BiPAP, CPAP, or high PEEP; give blood and products per the MHP, use vasopressors/inotropes as required; perform an early echo (TTE/TOE) for right heart dysfunction or pulmonary hypertension
Discuss with obstetrics: urgent delivery if antenatal; rule out other sources of haemorrhage (placenta, uterine rupture or tone, trauma); consider hysterectomy if bleeding is uncontrollable
Refer to ICU early — is ECMO a consideration? Does pulmonary hypertension need treatment?
thinking: AFE is rare but life-threatening — always consider it in your differential
Suggestive features: sudden agitation (non-compliance, pulling at lines), symptoms with no other clear explanation, onset during labour, delivery, or within 30 minutes of birth

Systems affected: general (restless, anxious, chest pain, vomiting); respiratory (hypoxia, bronchospasm, pulmonary oedema, ARDS — with pulmonary hypertension and right heart strain on investigation); cardiovascular (hypotension, chest pain, cardiac arrest — coagulopathy); neurological (headache, seizure, loss of consciousness — DIC); foetal (acute bradycardia)

drugs.bolus: 1 g slow push, repeat 30 min later
Metaraminol 1 mg; phenylephrine 100 mcg; ephedrine 9 mg; adrenaline 10–50 mcg

cards.title: High Airway Pressure
cards.priority: Systematic diagnosis
doing: Listen to the chest; watch for bilateral chest rise and fall
Switch to the bag — manually ventilate to confirm high pressure
Examine the EtCO₂ waveform for bronchospasm or a kinked ETT
Exclude light anaesthesia and inadequate muscle relaxation
Perform a systematic visual check: airway device (position/kinking) → filter → circuit → valves → ventilator
Check airway patency: suction the full length of the ETT or perform a bronchoscopic exam
If autoPEEP is suspected, watch for persistent expiratory flow at end-expiration; try disconnecting the circuit

If the problem is not identified: exclude the circuit (replace with an Ambu-bag, convert to TIVA if needed); exclude the filter (replace/remove); exclude the airway (replace the ETT, or convert an LMA to an ETT); if still unresolved, consider a patient problem
thinking: Consider the timing of the event — CVL insertion, position change, surgical event
Circuit causes: ventilator settings, kinked tube, valve failures, obstructed filter, O₂ flush failure
Airway causes: laryngospasm, tube position, tube size, blocked/kinked tube

Patient causes: chest wall rigidity, bronchospasm, anaphylaxis, pneumothorax, pneumoperitoneum, obesity, alveolar pathology (oedema, infection, ARDS, contusion), tracheal pathology (foreign body, secretions, tumour)

cards.title: Low SpO₂
cards.priority: Systematic diagnosis
doing: Check FiO₂ and turn to 100% O₂
Check patient colour, peripheral temperature, and probe position
Switch to the bag to test circuit integrity and lung compliance
Check the SpO₂ and EtCO₂ waveforms to guide diagnosis

If the EtCO₂ waveform is abnormal or absent: exclude a disconnected circuit, cardiac arrest, or low cardiac output; consider laryngospasm or bronchospasm (convert an LMA to an ETT); check airway position and patency, visualise the cords to rule out oesophageal intubation, look in the mouth for kinks/gastric contents, suction the ETT or bronchoscope; check ventilator mode/settings; ventilate via an Ambu-bag to exclude a ventilator/circuit/probe problem
If the EtCO₂ waveform is normal (circuit integrity intact): check fresh gas flow/FiO₂; exclude an endobronchial ETT; exclude pneumothorax (neck veins, chest rise, auscultate or ultrasound)
Work through the full diagnostic checklist to exclude other causes

Lung/breathing causes: apnoea/hypoventilation, atelectasis, pneumothorax, pulmonary oedema, sepsis/ARDS, contusion, pneumonia, interstitial lung disease
Circulatory causes: cardiac arrest, heart failure, anaphylaxis, embolism (pulmonary, air, CO₂, cement), hypothermia/poor peripheral circulation, methaemoglobinaemia (e.g. prilocaine)

doing: Quick check of patient monitors — Anaesthetist's ABCDE
If no EtCO₂ waveform, diagnose immediately: incorrect ETT placement — if in doubt, replace; severe bronchospasm — confirm the airway; check circuit and EtCO₂ sample-line connections

If low EtCO₂, first frequency gamble: cardiac or peri-arrest?; check the sampling line is securely connected and patent; examine the patient (airway position/patency, bilateral chest rise to rule out laryngospasm/bronchospasm); check the ventilator is switched on with correct tidal volume/respiratory rate
If the problem is not identified, work through the causes systematically
thinking: Even in cardiac arrest without CPR, EtCO₂ should still be recordable
If not urgent, correlate EtCO₂ with an ABG and PaCO₂
No EtCO₂: oesophageal intubation, no ventilation/no airway, cardiac arrest, circuit/sampling-line disconnection, ventilator failure or not switched on, apnoea
Decreased production: hypothermia, deep anaesthesia, low thyroid
Sampling dilution: high fresh gas flow, sampler placed incorrectly, dilution with air, circuit disconnected
Increased elimination: hyperventilation
Decreased CO₂ transport in blood: severe hypotension, anaphylaxis, cardiac arrest, air/pulmonary embolism, tamponade/tension pneumothorax
Decreased CO₂ diffusion in lung: low tidal volumes/dead space, laryngospasm, severe bronchospasm, ETT obstruction, endobronchial intubation

cards.title: Tachycardia
cards.priority: Differentiate sinus tachycardia from complex tachy-arrhythmia
doing: Check patient monitors — Anaesthetist's ABCDE
If there is diagnostic uncertainty and MAP <65 mmHg with HR >150, give a synchronised DC shock; may repeat up to 3 times and give amiodarone
Differentiate sinus tachycardia from complex tachy-arrhythmia: current surgical/pain stimulation; sinus rhythm?; QRS regularity?; QRS width?
If sinus tachycardia, consider causes

If complex tachy-arrhythmia, treat based on MAP: MAP <65 mmHg → synchronised DC shock; MAP >65 mmHg → manage by QRS width/regularity — Broad regular (VT/WPW: amiodarone; SVT with bundle block: treat as narrow), Broad irregular (AF with bundle block: treat as narrow; torsades: magnesium), Narrow regular (SVT: vagal manoeuvres → adenosine → beta-blocker), Narrow irregular (AF: beta-blocker or amiodarone)
Send an urgent blood gas — ensure high-normal K⁺ and Mg²⁺
thinking: Primary causes: IHD, cardiomyopathy, sick sinus syndrome, accessory conduction pathways, myocarditis, pericarditis, valvular disease, congenital heart disease
Secondary causes: hypovolaemia, anaesthesia depth, drugs (including drug error), pain, anaphylaxis, electrolyte abnormalities, cardiac tamponade, sepsis, thyroid storm, MH
Synchronised shock guide: AF/monomorphic VT 100→150→200 J; SVT/flutter 100→200 J; polymorphic VT/unstable 150→200 J

drugs.bolus: 6 mg, then 12 mg, then 18 mg, then consider other causes
Esmolol 10 mg titrated; metoprolol 2.5 mg boluses titrated (max 15 mg)

cards.title: Bradycardia
cards.priority: Treat if MAP <65 mmHg or evidence of poor perfusion
doing: Quick check of patient monitors — Anaesthetist's ABCDE
If MAP >65 mmHg, there is time to consider causes
If MAP <65 mmHg ± evidence of decreased perfusion: atropine; glycopyrrolate; ephedrine bolus titrated; adrenaline infusion; isoprenaline infusion; dopamine infusion

If drug toxicity/overdose: beta-blocker — as above plus high-dose insulin infusion and sodium bicarbonate if propranolol overdose; calcium-channel blocker — as for beta-blocker plus IV calcium chloride slow push (may repeat)

If severe refractory bradycardia, try external temporary pacing: attach defib/ECG leads, set to pacer mode, select rate 60/min, increase mA of output until capture (normally 65–100 mA), set final output 10 mA above capture, confirm pulse
If PEA occurs at any point, start CPR
thinking: Primary causes: athlete, IHD, AV block, pacemaker malfunction, cardiomyopathy, sick sinus syndrome, myocarditis, pericarditis, valvular disease, pulmonary hypertension
Secondary causes: vagal stimulation, drugs (error, overdose, anti-arrhythmics), electrolyte abnormality, low thyroid, hypothermia, raised ICP, cardiac tamponade, tension pneumothorax
Anaesthetic causes: vasopressors, volatile agents, suxamethonium, opioids, high/total spinal, anticholinesterases, hypoxia, auto-PEEP, MH, abnormal K⁺

drugs.infusion: dilute 1 mg (5 vials) into 50 mL, infuse 0–60 mL/hr
5 mg in 50 mL saline, infuse 0–20 mL/hr
100 mg in 50 mL saline, infuse 0–20 mL/hr

drugs.bolus: 50 mL slow push, repeat every 2 min (target pH 7.45–7.55)

drugs.infusion: 100 u actrapid in 50 mL saline at 35 mL/hr, with 10% dextrose at 250 mL/hr (monitor glucose & K⁺ every 30 min)

cards.title: Hypertension
cards.priority: Treat reversible causes first, then antihypertensives to SBP ~160 mmHg
doing: Quick check of patient monitors — Anaesthetist's ABCDE
Check accuracy of the reading — check equipment, including transducer height

Frequency gamble on common causes: painful surgical activity (give analgesia); recent drug infusions/doses for drug error (including LA with adrenaline); tourniquet time; bladder volume/fluids infused
Systematically work through possible causes
Once all reversible causes are addressed, consider IV antihypertensive agents to a systolic BP target of around 160 mmHg
thinking: Anaesthesia causes: too light, pain, drugs (consider error), hypoxia, hypercapnia, MH, non-patent/tissued IV line, A-line transducer height
Surgical causes: pneumoperitoneum, tourniquet, aortic clamping, carotid endarterectomy, baroreceptor stimulation

Patient-related causes: essential hypertension, rebound hypertension (beta-blocker stopped), full bladder, pre-eclampsia, renal disease, phaeochromocytoma (always give an alpha-blocker before a beta-blocker), thyroid storm, raised ICP

drugs.bolus: Esmolol 10 mg boluses titrated; metoprolol 2.5 mg boluses titrated (max 15 mg)
Labetalol (also a beta-blocker) 5 mg boluses titrated (max 100 mg); phentolamine 5–10 mg IV repeated every 5–15 min

drugs.notes: GTN sublingual spray or IV infusion 50 mg in 50 mL saline at 3 mL/hr, titrate; magnesium slow bolus 5 mL of 49.3%, repeat if required

cards.title: Hypotension
cards.priority: Assess severity; treat reversible causes
doing: Check patient monitors — Anaesthetist's ABCDE
Check accuracy of the reading — check equipment, including transducer height
Assess severity: visualise the patient, check the ECG and EtCO₂/SpO₂ waveforms
No cardiac output or critical MAP: start CPR
MAP <65 mmHg with concern: leg elevation; rapid fluid infusion ± readiness to transfuse blood; IV vasopressors or inotropes
Consider reversible causes — frequency gamble on common causes, then systematically work through each cause in turn
Consider echo (if skilled) to differentiate causes, and other invasive monitoring (e.g. pulse pressure/stroke volume variation from an arterial line, cardiac index monitoring)
thinking: Always suspect concealed haemorrhage, particularly with scope surgery or after drug administration/surgical events
Decreased preload: blood loss/hypovolaemia, increased intrathoracic pressure, decreased venous return (IVC compression, position, pneumoperitoneum), tamponade/tension pneumothorax, embolism
Decreased contractility: drugs including volatiles, IHD, cardiomyopathy, myocarditis, arrhythmia, valvular disease
Decreased afterload: drugs (vasodilators including anaesthetic agents, opioids, antihypertensives), neuraxial techniques, sepsis, tourniquet/clamp release, anaphylaxis, Addisonian crisis, low thyroid
Equipment/human factors: artefact or failure, wrong transducer height, wrong NIBP cuff size, drug error
PPV/SVV >12% suggests hypovolaemia (only valid if intubated, paralysed, tidal volume >8 mL/kg, sinus rhythm, normal abdominal pressure). Normal cardiac index is >2.6 L/min/m²

drugs.bolus: Metaraminol 0.5 mg (10 mcg/kg); phenylephrine 100 mcg; ephedrine 9 mg (0.25 mg/kg); adrenaline 10–50 mcg

drugs.infusion: 5 mg in 50 mL saline, infuse 0–20 mL/hr

cards.title: Failure to Wake
cards.priority: Generally not a crisis — use the time to consider causes
doing: Airway: ensure a patent, unobstructed airway
Breathing: ensure an established respiratory pattern; check SpO₂; check the EtCO₂ trace and value
Cardiovascular: ensure normal HR, MAP, and ECG

Drugs: review all drugs given during the anaesthetic — check muscle relaxation with a nerve stimulator and give a reversal agent; consider timing and infusions of all agents; consider drug errors and interactions; consider patient factors (renal/hepatic failure, elderly)
Neurological: check pupils; apply BIS for signs of seizure (detects frontal lobe seizures only); consider the need for CT
Metabolic: send a blood gas — check PaO₂, PaCO₂, sodium, glucose
Temperature: ensure core temperature is >30 °C
Systematically work through all remaining causes
thinking: Drug causes: analgesic agents (opioids, alpha-2 agonists); anaesthetic agents (volatile, propofol); muscle relaxants (suxamethonium apnoea, inadequate reversal); sedatives (benzodiazepines, anticholinergics, antihistamines, antidopaminergics); magnesium toxicity
Metabolic causes: abnormal blood glucose, abnormal sodium, raised urea
Respiratory failure: hypoxia or hypercapnia from decreased central drive (stroke, COPD), lung disease (PE, ARDS), or muscle power issues (obesity)
Neurological causes: stroke (infarct, bleed, embolism); seizure (non-convulsive status epilepticus or post-ictal); local anaesthetic toxicity
Other uncommon causes: central anticholinergic syndrome, dissociative coma, thyroid failure, toxicity of other CNS drugs
Hypothermia

drugs.bolus: PTC >2 = 4 mg/kg (70 kg = 280 mg); >T2 = 2 mg/kg (70 kg = 140 mg)
Neostigmine 2.5 mg (50 mcg/kg) with glycopyrrolate 500 mcg (10 mcg/kg); repeat at 15 min

drugs.notes: No reversal agent available — continue anaesthesia/sedation and refer to ICU
